title: High-Frequency Trading around Large Institutional Orders
abstract: Liquidity suppliers lean against the wind. We analyze whether high-frequency traders (HFTs) lean against large institutional orders that execute through a series of child orders. The alternative is HFTs trading with the wind, that is, in the same direction. We find that HFTs initially lean against these orders but eventually change direction and take positions in the same direction for the most informed institutional orders. Our empirical findings are consistent with investors trading strategically on their information. When deciding trade intensity, they seem to trade off higher speculative profits against higher risk of being detected and preyed on by HFTs.

article.introduction:
Migration to electronic trading createda new type of market participant: high-frequency traders (HFTs). The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) characterized such traders as “professional traders acting in proprietary capacity” who use “extraordinarily high-speed and sophisticated computer programs for generating, routing, and executing orders” and end the trading day “in as close to a flat position as possible.” HFTs entered securities markets in the late 2000s, appearing first in equity markets. Their market participation, in percentage terms, is typically a few deciles (SEC (2010), European Securities and Markets Authority (2014)).
High-frequency trading has triggered a great deal of academic study, particularly after NASDAQ released data that label HFT trades. The evidence is by and large favorable on HFTs, emphasizing reduced bid-ask spreads and increased price efficiency, although evidence on how HFTs relate to “excess” volatility, such as in flash crashes, is mixed. Biais and Foucault (2014), O'Hara (2015), and Menkveld (2016) provide survey articles on the young and rapidly growing HFT literature.1
Notwithstanding, we know little about how HFT relates to trading by an important group of end users of securities markets: institutional investors. Retail investors benefit from a smaller bid-ask spread, since there is generally enough depth at the best quote to execute their order. Institutional investors, however, need to “work their order” by splitting a parent order into small child orders that are fed to the market sequentially. Institutional investors care about “implementation shortfall,” that is, the average price at which the entire order executes relative to the price that prevailed when it started executing. In other words, they care about how far they pushed the price away from them while executing. Importantly, institutional investors care aboutcumulativeprice impact rather than the half-spread paid on, for example, a single market order. Some have expressed the concern that trading costs have increased and attribute this to the presence of HFTs.2
Objective. In this paper, our objective is to document how HFTs trade, while an institutional order executes through a series of child trades, and to provide an economic narrative grounded in theoretical studies. Three such studies speak directly to our empirical setting. First, as suggested by Grossman and Miller (1988), HFTs could bemarket makerswho take the other side of the order. For example, they would be selling in the period when a buy order executes. Second, as Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2005) suggest, they could engage inpredatory trading, buying along with the institutional order initially to then sell when the buy order is almost finished executing, essentially riding the (transitory) price-pressure wave. Third, as Yang and Zhu (2017) suggest, they might need time to learn that the buy order is executing toback-runon it later by also buying.3Taking such a long position relatively late only makes money, however, if the order is information-motivated (as opposed to an uninformed order that seeks liquidity). These three studies offer distinct predictions for how HFTs trade as intermediaries while an institutional order executes and for the way in which prices respond (see SectionI).
We take the predicted patterns to the data. The empirical analysis is based on a sample that combines proprietary data on order executions by institutional investors with public HFT trade data. The sample runs from January 1, 2011 through March 31, 2013 and covers trading in Swedish index stocks. The order execution data were provided by four large institutional investors (APG, DNB, NBIM, and Swedbank Robur) and include 801,341 child trades. We construct parent orders, which we refer to as meta orders, by stringing together child trades by a single institution in a single stock, possibly over multiple days (for details, see SectionII.B). The final sample consists of 5,136 such meta orders that, on average, contain 156 child trades that execute over a bit more than four hours. Not surprisingly, we find that these orders are directional in the sense that all child trades in a single order are either almost exclusively buys or almost exclusively sells. Finally, institutional orders tend to be large averaging $2.2 million, or 3.88% when expressed relative to average daily volume.
An important benefit of this particular sample is that during the period considered, HFTs had to reveal their trades on NASDAQ OMX,4the dominant market at the time with a market share of two-thirds for the local index stocks.5We select Europe's largest high-frequency trading firms according toFinancial News: Citadel, Flow Traders, Getco, IAT, IMC, Knight, Optiver, Spire, Susquehanna, and Virtu.6Collectively, the participation rate of these firms in NASDAQ OMX trades is almost a third in our sample.
Findings. The empirical analysis yields three main findings. First, the pattern of HFT trading fits none of the three theoretical studies perfectly. We find that HFTs as a group appear to lean against the wind when an order starts executing, but if execution lasts more than seven hours (possibly multiple days), they reverse course and trade with the wind. Indeed, they not only close out their position, but they also actuallybuilda speculative position in the direction of the order. For buy orders, for example, HFTs initially sell but after seven hours start buying, and they buy lots more than they initially sold. A potential concern with this result is that HFTs could have entered offsetting positions in alternative markets or in highly correlated securities. We consider this concern unlikely, however, because perfectly correlated securities are hard to find for single stocks, and NASDAQ OMX is by far the largest equity exchange for Swedish stocks.
A more pressing concern is that this result could be mechanical: to the extent that institutional investors and HFTs both respond to certain market conditions, any correlation in their trading patterns would be driven by a third factor. To rule out such an explanation, we create a placebo sample in which none of the institutional investors were active yet market conditions were similar (in terms of volume, idiosyncratic return, market return, volatility, and [market] order imbalance). We find evidence of HFT against-wind trading in the placebo sample, but not with-wind trading. With-wind trading therefore seems to be robustly related to the presence of an institutional order. This finding motivates the remainder of the empirical analysis and the overall message of the paper.
Late HFT with-wind trading is consistent with both predatory trading and back-running. As alluded to earlier, the two theories can be distinguished by analyzing the information content of institutional orders. This is where we turn next.
Our second main finding is that institutional orders appear to be mostly information-motivated, particularly those with long-lasting executions that HFTs eventually trade along with. A couple of findings support this claim. First, on days when institutions trade, they typically trade only one or a few stocks. On the spectrum from stock-picking based on information signals to uninformed trading of the market portfolio, institutions seem to be closer to the stock-picking end. Analysis of the informational content of their orders based on the extent to which prices rebound after their final child trade supports the informed trading view. More specifically, the information component dominates the transitory component in their order's overall price impact (OPI). Finally, in the cross-section of orders, this information component is larger for orders that HFTs trade with-wind on. This association is economically large as the average permanent price impact (PPI) rises from 6 basis points to 15 with a one-standard-deviation increase in with-wind flow.
Contribution to Recent Literature. Our paper is related to three recent papers on trading by institutional investors and HFTs. Korajczyk and Murphy (2018) study how HFTs trade around large orders using a Canadian sample. They assign HFT labels based on trader behavior and infer large orders from aggregate flow by broker-client account. They document against-wind flow initially and with-wind flow later in the course of large order executions, and they further find that the implementation shortfall paid on large orders is higher for “stressful trades,” that is, trades for which HFT liquidity provision is predicted to be lower.
Our study differs from Korajczyk and Murphy (2018) in three ways. First, we identify end-user net flow, as opposed to client flow per broker. End users often use multiple brokers to execute their orders (Linnainmaa and Saar (2012)). Second, we observe HFT names in the sample and therefore do not need to rely on inference based on behavior. Such inference is necessarily imperfect since speed, for example, is an important HFT characteristic that is hard to observe in trade and quote data. Third, they focus on HFT liquidity provision, whereas our focus is on with-wind trading by HFTs, which is more robust in our sample. We develop an economic narrative and support it with further analysis (e.g., show that institutional orders are information-motivated).
Next, Tong (2015) relates the average implementation shortfall to high-frequency trading intensity for a U.S. equity sample. The author averages across all institutional investors in the Abel/Noser data set and documents that high shortfall days coincide with days of high HFT intensity, both for HFT “market-making” and HFT “directional trading.” The benefit of our data set is that it hasintradaytime stamps (as opposed to daily time stamps) and identifies HFTs by name (as opposed to an exchange-labeled category). This allows us to study exactly how HFTs trade during the lifetime of an order.
The third related paper is Hirschey (2014), who documents that HFT aggressive flow in this second predicts non-HFT aggressive flow in the next 30 seconds. We extend Hirschey's (2014) findings in two ways. First, we focus on institutional investor flow, which is a subset of non-HFT flow—the latter also contains flow by retail investors and other intermediaries, such as proprietary trading by sell-side banks. Second, our results suggest that HFTs respond to investor flow at low frequencies. We believe that HFTs should be thought of more broadly than as machines engaged in submillisecond speed races. Indeed, we suspect that their superior information technology enables them to generate better signals from “big data” assembled over multiple hours and trade on them profitably.
Some Words of Caution. Our study faces several limitations and we therefore caution against overinterpretation of the results. First, we do not claim causality. All of the results are either unconditional or conditional (regression) associations. We nevertheless believe that the interaction between a set of sophisticated intermediaries and large institutional investors should be of general interest. Moreover, the empirical patterns being more supportive of one theory (i.e., Yang and Zhu (2017)) over others help us better understand the functioning of securities markets.
Second, while HFTs appear to trade with-wind on institutional orders, they might not be the only ones to do so. In particular, the data that we employ identify HFTs, but not other important intermediaries such as the proprietary trading desks of sell-side banks or hedge funds. In our data, the order flow of such intermediaries is part of the aggregate order flow at the market level.
Third, with-wind trading by intermediaries likely plagued investors long before the rise of HFTs (Harris (1997)). Intermediaries have existed for centuries. One could argue that back-running was even easier in human-intermediated markets because broker-dealers observed their client flow directly, since it had to pass through their hands. They were not allowed to trade on this information, but enforcement was extremely difficult (traders could tip each other off). Our contribution is simply to document with-wind trading on institutional orders without claiming that it is somehow unique to HFTs operating in electronic markets. Indeed, there could very well belessof it now. Finally, in a broader sense, cream-skimming could also be interpreted as with-wind trading. For example, a New York Stock Exchange specialist would improve the price only if he suspected that the order originated from an uninformed investor, in which case he would raise the price impact for informed investors in equilibrium (Rock (1990)). Another example is dealers paying for retail order flow (Easley, Kiefer, and O'Hara (1996)).
Fourth, with-wind trading is not necessarily costly to investors as a group. If the HFT sector is competitive, HFTs could willingly incur a (net) loss when initially trading against the wind. Doing so, they might discover large long-lasting orders on which they can then profitably back-run. This implies a transfer from large long-lasting orders to small institutional orders or to retail orders.
The paper proceeds as follows. SectionIdevelops the theoretical motivation of the study. SectionIIdescribes the data. SectionIIIanalyzes HFT net flow during the execution of institutional orders, and SectionIVrelates HFT net flow to predatory trading and back-running. SectionVshows that with-wind trading is consistent with strategic interplay between HFTs and institutional investors. SectionVIpresents results of robustness checks, and SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Price and Trade Patterns as Predicted:
In this section, we summarize several priors for trading by HFTs around large institutional orders. Figure1depicts the expected price pattern and HFT cumulative net flow over the lifetime of an institutional buy order and the period shortly thereafter. The three panels correspond to market-making (Grossman and Miller (1988)), predatory trading (Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2005)), and back-running (Yang and Zhu (2017)). These patterns are briefly discussed below.
Grossman and Miller (1988) predict that market-making HFTs sell to an institutional buy order and close out their position in the long run. The price rises when the institutional investor buys to compensate risk-averse market-makers for supplying immediacy. Market-makers will off-load their position at fundamental valueseventuallybut incur price risk on their inventory in the short run. Selling to the institutional buyer at a (temporarily) elevated price compensates them for such risk. The identifying features of this are therefore that (1) HFTs sell throughout the lifetime of the institutional buy order and (2) the price impact is transitory (see Panel A of Figure1).
Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2005) model strategic trading by predators who are aware that an institutional investor is forced to trade for liquidity reasons. Their findings imply that predatory HFTs first trade along with an institutional buy order, and thus add to price pressure, but eventually turn around and sell to the institutional order. Since HFTs enter positions at a price below the price at which they exit, they earn a positive profit in expectation. The institutional investor suffers, since price pressure is stronger than what it otherwise would have been. Predatory trading is front-running in a general sense. The key features of this are that (1) HFTs trade along with the institutional order initially and then unwind their position at elevated prices, and (2) the price impact is transitory (see Panel B of Figure1).
Yang and Zhu (2017) propose back-running as intermediaries learning about the execution of a privately informed order. If HFTs could sniff out these orders, they would trade along with them while these informed orders continue to execute until the privately known fundamental value is reached. If HFTs could then unwind their position after completion of the institutional order, they would earn positive expected profits. Institutional investors suffer from HFT back-running as HFTs effectively take part in their “informational rent,” that is, prices are pushed to fundamental values more quickly when HFTs join the trade. The key features are that (1) buying by HFTs is delayed due to an initial learning period, and (2) the price impact is permanent (see Panel C of Figure1).
article.II. Trade Environment and Data:
This section describes the NASDAQ OMX trading environment and presents the public and proprietary data sets. The public data set contains trades with exchange member identities that are used to identify HFT trades. The proprietary data set contains the child order execution records of four large institutional investors. These investors cannot be identified in the public data, since they are not exchange members. They use brokers to route their orders to the exchange. Both data sets correspond to trading in the 30 Swedish index stocks from January 1, 2011 through March 31, 2013.
NASDAQ OMX runs mostly like a standard limit-order market to trade its Swedish stocks. The most notable idiosyncratic feature is ex post trade transparency on who traded. Trade records that are revealed in real time contain the usual fields, in particular, a time stamp in milliseconds, a transaction price, and a transaction quantity. However, at the end of each trading day, NASDAQ OMX also reveals who was trading with whom for each transaction. This identification is carried out at the exchange member level and therefore does not reveal end users. HFTs, banks, and brokers are exchange members, but not institutional investors who trade through banks or brokers. A total of 89 exchange members were active in our sample.
At the time of our sample, NASDAQ OMX faced competition from other trading venues such as multilateral trading facilities and dark pools. Its market share for exchange-traded volume was 65%. The most active rival exchange, Chi-X, had 20% market share. The remaining 15% was shared by four other exchanges. An important caveat of this study is that we do not observe the trades by HFTs on these alternative markets. We revisit this issue in the robustness analysis presented in SectionVI.
Two sets of public data are used in this study, namely, equity transactions with member identification and index future returns. Both are obtained from the Thomson Reuters Tick History. As mentioned in the introduction, we use member identities to identify the aggregate net flow of the 10 largest HFTs.
The proprietary data consist of the child order transactions of four large institutional investors highly active in Swedish index stocks. The data contain detailed NASDAQ OMX execution information, including a time stamp (to the second), price, direction, and quantity.
As parent order information is missing, the best we can do is infer them from child order executions. We do so by stringing these child trades together into meta orders and creating observations at the stock-period institution level. First, for each stock and each day, we aggregate all child order executions by a single institution into stock-day-institution orders. Then, following Korajczyk and Murphy (2018), we further aggregate these orders across days if there is a child trade in the last half-hour of one day followed by a child trade in the first half-hour the next day. It turns out that 577 out of the 5,136 orders span multiple days. We refer to institutional meta orders as institutional orders in the remainder of the paper for brevity.
We employ two filters to construct the sample that is used in all of our analyses. First, we exclude institutional orders with low directionality, since our focus is on orders that built a position over the course of the day (as opposed to intraday trading strategies). Directionality is based on net flow and defined as the absolute value of the difference between buy and sell volumes, divided by total volume (all in shares).7Orders with directionality below 0.90 are removed from the sample. This filter removes 11.5% of orders. We find that 95% of the remaining orders consist of either purely buys or purely sells. Second, we winsorize HFT net flow at the 1% and 99% levels. This filter addresses extreme outliers in HFT net flow.
TableIpresents summary statistics for the 5,136 stock-period institution observations. Statistics are reported for the full sample as well as for institutional buy and sell orders separately.
Institutional orders are large and long-lasting. The average order is $2.2 million or 3.88% of average daily volume, and consists of 156 child trades over 4.26 hours, on average. The fraction of orders executed through market orders (as opposed to limit orders) is 40.6%, which we consider close to the 34.5% documented for ITG Posit data in the United States (O'Hara (2015)). The average implementation shortfall is $4,294 or 8.27 basis points when expressed relative to order size. Institutional sell orders are somewhat larger on average and execute over a shorter period of time.
In the period in which it executes, an institutional order is a sizable part of the market. Its net volume for buy or sell orders is $2.2 million, which compares to overall volume of $61.2 million over the same period (i.e., it is 3.6% of overall volume), and its net number of trades is 156 out of 5,507 in the market (2.8%). Directionality is at least 0.99 on average, which is well above the 0.90 threshold used to filter the data (see SectionII.B).
While the institutional order executes, HFTs are highly active. Specifically, they trade $13.1 million, or 21% of overall volume. This volume is executed through, on average, 1,514 trades. Note that this suggests that the average trade size is $8,652 ($13.1 million/1,514), which is about half of what the same ratio yields for institutional child order size: $14,100. Directionality in HFT trades is only 8%. The relatively small trade size and low directionality are in line with the well-known HFT preference for keeping inventory at a minimum (SEC (2010)).
Inspection of start and end times of the institutional orders shows that these times are quite evenly dispersed throughout the day with two exceptions (see Figure IA.1 in the Internet Appendix):8First, end times tend to concentrate at the close, with 31% of the orders ending in the last 15 minutes of the trading day. Second, start and end times peak between 15:45 and 16:00, which is right after U.S. equity markets open. These observations led us to flag these orders in panel regressions below by adding dummies.
In the Internet Appendix, an additional set of summary statistics show that HFTs mean-revert their position substantially within the day, but also across days. In particular, Table IA.1 shows that the directionality (i.e., |B-S|/(B+S)) of HFTs for stock-days, stock-weeks, and stock-months is 0.084, 0.048, and 0.028, respectively. These numbers suggest that HFTs are close to flat at the end of the day, but not entirely so—part of the position that they accumulate within a day they smooth out across weeks or even months. Not mean-reverting entirely to zero is perhaps not surprising, given that we do not see their trading outside of NASDAQ OMX (see SectionII.A). HFTs might also hedge their positions with correlated assets to obtain a flat position in terms of risk. The table shows that the directionality of institutional investors, in contrast, is much larger across days, weeks, and months, at 0.99, 0.91, and 0.83, respectively.
article.III. HFT Net Flow during the Execution o:
In this section, we investigate whether HFTs lean against the wind or go with the wind while an institutional order executes through a series of child orders. We also construct a placebo sample to study whether HFT behavior is associated with the presence of an institutional order, or is simply driven by the market conditions that prevailed at the time the institution implemented its order.
In this subsection, we plot how HFT net flow develops over the lifetime of an institutional order. We track HFT net flow cumulatively, starting from the first institutional child trade. Snapshots are taken every 30 minutes. At the time of the snapshot, HFT net flow is averaged over all institutional orders that are still executing (i.e., the final child trade has not arrived yet). This implies that, for the first half-hour, for example, the average is taken across short- and long-lasting orders. We consider this a reasonable approach, since HFTs are unlikely to know the order duration ex ante (a view supported by the data, as shown below in TableIII). If the average net flow is negative, then HFTs lean against the order (i.e., against the wind). If the average net flow is positive, then HFTs trade along with the order (i.e., with the wind). We do an equivalent analysis for institutional sell orders.
Figure2plots the results for buy orders (left panel) and sell orders (right panel). The plots show that HFTs lean against buy orders during the first seven hours of execution, since they go short for approximately $20,000. This result is statistically significant only in the first five hours, however, as indicated by the solid dots. The right panel shows that HFTs lean against sell orders only in the first two hours. This result is statistically weaker, significant only at the 10% level.
Strikingly, HFTs turn around and go with the order if it lasts more than seven hours for buys and more than two hours for sells. This result turns statistically significant only if the order lasts longer than seven hours. HFT cumulative net flow continues to grow until it plateaus at around $300,000 with-wind. It reaches this level after about 20 hours for institutional buys and 15 hours for institutional sells.
Overall, it seems that with-wind trading is slightly stronger for sell orders since initial against-wind trading is weaker and the large with-wind position is reached earlier. We suspect that this is due to the higher execution intensity of sell orders. Indeed, TableIreveals that sell orders are 38% larger and are executed an hour faster, on average. This finding is not all that surprising as investors might rationally choose to specialize (Van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp (2009)), in which case they should be better informed about the stocks they own (and consider selling) than the stocks they do not own (but consider buying).9
The dashed green line indicates the number of institutional orders still executing at each point in time (righty-axis). For buy orders, we observe significant with-wind flow for the 500 orders that are still executing after seven hours. This is about a sixth of all buy orders. For sell orders, there is significant with-wind flow for 200 orders, which is about a tenth of all sell orders.
The analysis in this section raises new questions, two of which are answered by additional analyses reported in the Internet Appendix. First, do institutions execute their orders linearly over time, or is there front- or backloading? Figure IA.2 in the Internet Appendix shows that on average investors execute fairly linearly, for both buy and sell orders. Sell orders, however, execute more intensively, since their slope is about 50% larger in magnitude. This is not surprising since, as noted earlier, sell orders are larger and execute over a shorter horizon. Second, can HFTs be treated as a single group, or do different HFTs follow different strategies in response to institutional orders? Table IA.2 in the Internet Appendix shows that if, in aggregate, HFTs trade against the wind over the lifetime of an order, each individual HFT trades against the wind on average as well. We find a similar result for with-wind trading. Having said that, we note that there is some heterogeneity across HFTs in terms of how intensively they trade against wind or with wind. The overall finding of relative homogeneity for these HFTs is somewhat remarkable, given that the HFT literature discusses a variety of HFT strategies (see, for example, Menkveld (2016) for a literature survey).
We create a placebo sample to identify whether the HFT flow pattern is more closely related to the institutional order or to market conditions that prevailed at the time of the order.
A “nearest-neighbor” matching procedure is used to identify the placebo days. We follow Davies and Kim (2009, p. 183) with one modification, namely, we measure the distance in standard deviation units as opposed to percentages.11
Note that the placebo analysis controls for a momentum-based explanation of HFT behavior. If institutional orders have a price impact and if HFTs trade on momentum, then a with-wind pattern occurs naturally. However, if the with-wind HFT pattern is present in the original sampleminusthe placebo sample (i.e., in the differential), then this alternative explanation becomes less likely. Both samples are constructed to have the same price pattern in terms of market return and idiosyncratic return.
TableIIpresents trade statistics for both the (treated) institutional order sample and the placebo sample. It shows that the match seems reasonable in terms of distance. Its value is small and evenly distributed across all matched variables.
Figure3plots HFT net flow for the treated and the placebo samples. The plot for the treated sample is the same as in Figure2. The only difference is that the dots now denote significance of HFT net flow tested against placebo HFT net flow, as opposed to testing against zero. In other words, the graph corresponds to a test on whether thedifferentialbetween the two HFT net flows is zero.
In the placebo sample, HFT net flow mostly leans against the wind: it is negative for buy orders throughout and positive for sell orders in the first eight hours. When compared to the placebo sample, treated buy orders show significantly more against-wind trading in the first five hours and more with-wind trading after eight hours. For sell orders, results are weaker, and we only see significantly more with-wind trading between five and seven hours. For sells, there seems to be a with-wind pattern in the placebo sample as well, but this applies to a small sample of orders that are still executing. When pooling buys and sells into pseudo-buy orders,12Figure IA.3 in the Internet Appendix shows that the treated sample has significantly more against-wind flow in the first three hours and more with-wind flow after seven hours. Taken together, these findings suggest that some of the against-wind pattern is due to market conditions, whereas the with-wind pattern seems truly associated with the presence of an institutional order.
One interpretation of these placebo results is that HFTs use a market-making strategy in normal market conditions. In the treated sample, institutional orders have a price impact (see TableII), that is, the average idiosyncratic return is positive for buy orders and negative for sell orders. Given that the placebo sample is matched on this variable, it seems that HFTs trade against price changes: they sell when prices go up and buy when prices go down, at least initially. The wedge between the two lines suggests that HFTs switch from market-making to speculation when they detect a persistent, directional long-lasting order. They mostly stick to market marking in the placebo sample (except for institutional sell orders that last longer than eight hours, in which case the placebo pattern becomes erratic as the number of orders still executing is less than 100; see Figure2).
It is important to stress that while there are no order executions by the four identified institutional investors in the placebo sample, other institutional investors could have executed similar orders. We are not overly worried though, since this would bias against us finding anything. In other words, this observation implies that not only are the results that wedofind really there, but they also underestimate the true strength of the effect.
The patterns that we have documented thus far suggest that HFTs need time to detect large, long-lasting orders to trade with-wind on them. However, the empirical patterns above are based on average HFT positions, and thus do not rule out the possibility that HFTs detect the subset of large, long-lasting orders right from the start. Moreover, even if they do need time to learn about these orders, it is not clearhowthey learn about them. These observations raise the question of whether particular order or market characteristics somehow reveal their presence. We address these questions next.
We first analyze HFT net flow patterns over time for subsamples based on order size and duration. Evidence that the patterns are similar for these subsamples, and in particular around the start of the orders, would imply that HFTs do need time to detect the presence of large, long-lasting institutional orders.
The results are reported in TableIII. Two observations stand out. First, whether we split the sample into small and large orders, we find that HFTs trade against-wind initially for both, but only significantly so for buy orders. The sell-order insignificance is not surprising, given that there is no such significance in the full sample either. (We report only the odd hours to save space; the even ones show a similar pattern.) These observations hold irrespective of whether the order size is measured relative to a stock's average daily volume (ADV) or is measured simply as the order's dollar value. One could even argue that the initial against-wind pattern is stronger for large orders and more significant.
Second, the initial against-wind pattern also holds for both subsamples when they are based on order duration as opposed to size. Long-lasting orders are defined as those that last longer than four hours (half a trading day). The result is again significant only for buy orders. Together, these results suggest that HFTs cannot distinguish between large or long-lasting orders early in their lifetime.
Given that HFTs need time to learn, what order characteristics or market conditions seem conducive to detection? We perform two regressions to shed light on this question. The dependent variable is either signed HFT net flow over the lifetime of the order, or a dummy variable that is equal to one for the tercile of institutional orders for which HFTs traded strongly with-wind. The latter regression is a Probit. The explanatory variables capture either order or market characteristics. Order characteristics include size, duration, buy or sell, the use of market orders, and time dummies that identify whether an order starts or ends around the U.S. open as well as whether it ends in the closing auction. Market characteristics include volatility, turnover, and a market fragmentation measure. TableIVpresents the regression results as well as a detailed description of these variables.
The regression results show that whether HFTs collectively trade with-wind on an order is hard to predict. The explanatory variables are mostly insignificant, with the following exceptions. Longer duration orders are more likely to show HFT with-wind trading, which might simply mean that a long and persistent imbalance in the market is harder to attribute to “normal” market conditions. With-wind trading also seems to be more prevalent on high-turnover days.13With-wind trading is less likely when institutional orders start at the U.S. open or end at the U.S. close. These results imply that detection is harder during such informationally intensive periods, or taking a position is more costly during these disproportionately volatile periods. Finally, with-wind trading appears more likely for institutional sell orders (as opposed to buy orders). As mentioned in our discussion of Figure2, this might be due to the fact that when investors sell, they trade more intensively than when they buy. This would arguably raise detection risk for sell orders.
The general observation of with-wind trading being hard to predict might not be that surprising if one thinks of the trading patterns as reflecting the interplay between institutional investors and HFTs. The latter benefit from learning about an institutional order executing by trading with-wind on it. This might hurt investors, who respond by trying to camouflage their trades. If they do so successfully, it is hard to predict HFT behavior with the standard explanatory variables (i.e., investors might even randomize order-specific variables). To put it more strongly, if one of these variables strongly predicted HFT with-wind trading, then investors would have a strong incentive to adjust their strategy. We develop this interpretation of investor-HFT interplay further in SectionV.
article.IV. Predatory Trading or Back-Running?:
The patterns documented in the previous section on how HFTs trade around institutional orders do not unequivocally support one of three theoretical patterns summarized in Figure1: market-making, predatory trading, and back-running. In particular, the HFT against-wind pattern is consistent with market-making, but it is not yet clear whether the with-wind pattern is a sign of predatory trading or back-running. To answer this question, in this section, we exploit an important distinguishing feature between the two. Specifically, predatory trading hypothesizes that an uninformed investor trades to satisfy a pressing liquidity need, whereas back-running assumes that the investor trades to capitalize on a private (informational) signal. We employ a standard microstructure technique to assess whether at least some institutional orders are information-motivated, and, if so, whether these are the orders that HFT back-run on.
Before turning to a full-blown price impact analysis, some exploratory analysis is useful. If the institutions considered are uninformed investors trading for liquidity reasons, one would expect them to trade the market portfolio. If instead they trade on private signals, then one would expect themnotto trade the market portfolio (as the market is more cheaply traded through index futures), but rather trade small sets of stocks or even a single stock. This broad distinction suggests computing how many stocks (names) an institution trades on days that it trades.
Figure4plots a histogram of the number of names an institution trades when it trades. Trading the market portfolio would entail trading all 30 constituent names. The histogram reveals that none of the institutions ever traded the entire market portfolio—far from it. They most often traded a single stock only. On average, they traded 5.6 names, with a standard deviation of 5.3. We next turn to a price impact analysis to study whether such trading could be interpreted as stock picking based on information.
To measure the informational content of institutional orders, we extrapolate from the standard microstructure spread decomposition approach (Glosten (1987), Hendershottt, Jones, and Menkveld (2011)). This decomposition is used to identify the information in asingletrade. This is done by simply waiting long enough for the transitory component in the trade's price impact to disappear, which is often taken to be five minutes. More specifically, the spread decomposition measures the effective spread of a (market) buy trade as its immediate price impact: trade price minus the midquote price that prevailed just before the execution, the pretrade midquote. This trade's (permanent) adverse-selection component is the midquote five minutes later minus the pretrade midquote. The residual (transitory) component is referred to as the trade's realized spread. Sell trades are pooled with buy trades by multiplying prices by minus one and thus making them pseudo-buys. Estimates of the effective spread and its two components rely on averaging them across a large sample of trades.
Figure5depicts the information content of institutional orders sorted by terciles of HFT cumulative net flow, calculated from order start to end. The figure shows that the information content of institutional orders is large on average, and larger for those orders for which HFTs trade with-wind. The OPI is 8.5 basis points on average, with 8.1 basis points for the tercile of against-wind orders and 11.4 basis points for the tercile of with-wind orders. The permanent price impact (5.7) is larger than the transitory impact (2.8). Interestingly, this wedge is larger for with-wind orders, which suggests that in the cross-section of orders, HFTs trade with-wind on the more informed orders. However, this finding might be driven by alternative factors, for example, order size, and thus our next regression controls for the effect of various covariates.15
TableVpresents results of the price impact regressions and shows that the positive correlation between PPI and HFT with-wind trading is robust. The main explanatory variables of interest in these regressions are with-wind and against-wind HFT cumulative net flow. The with-wind variable is defined as HFT cumulative net flow if (over the lifetime of the order) it carries the same sign as the institutional order, and zero otherwise. The against-wind variable is defined analogously, such that total HFT flow equals with-wind flow minus against-wind flow. Standard covariates are added as controls.
The OPI regressions show that the coefficient on HFT with-wind flow is positive, regardless of whether these variables are expressed in relative terms or dollar terms. Rerunning these regressions for the two components of OPI separately reveals that PPI drives this result. Stronger with-wind HFT flow is associated with more informed orders in the cross-section. This association is economically large, as a one-standard-deviation increase in with-wind flow implies a 9.28 basis points increase in PPI, which is high given that the overall average PPI is 5.69 basis points.
The regressions further show that the consistently significant covariate is order size. Its coefficient is significantly positive for both OPI and PPI, and is insignificant for TPI. This is consistent with institutional investors using their private information strategically, trading larger quantities when they have stronger signals. Note further that the with-wind flow's positive association with the information content of orders (PPI) is not simply an artifact of with-wind activity forlargerorders—the regression analysis shows that when size is controlled for, the positive association remains.
If the institutional order ends in the half-hour after the opening of the U.S. markets, OPI is significantly lower. This is likely due to higher overall trading activity in this period and improved liquidity, as the TPI regression suggests that most of the reduction comes from the transitory component.
Taken together, the findings in this section are consistent with HFTs back-running on informed institutional orders. Not only are the orders in our sample largely informed, but the with-wind activity is strongest for the most informed orders among them. We caution that our results do not reject the predatory trading hypothesis in general. Predatory trading requires stressed orders (e.g., forced liquidations) and is therefore more likely for orders by highly leveraged institutions (e.g., hedge funds).16Such institutions are not part of our sample, however, which consists of orders by two mutual funds, a sovereign wealth fund and a pension fund.
article.V. Putting It All Together: An Economic:
Above analysis documented that HFTs trade against-wind early in the lifetime of institutional orders, turning to with-wind trading on the most informed orders later in their execution. In this final section, we develop an economic narrative that rationalizes the with-wind trading by HFTs. We first develop predictions based on economic reasoning. We then conduct further empirical analysis to verify whether these predictions hold in the data.
These expressions lead to the following insights. First, if the price at the end of the meta-order execution reflects its information perfectly (as in, e.g., Kyle (1985)), it follows thatβ̂T=1andα̂T=0. Note further thatα̂0=v, as no private information has entered the price yet at the start of execution, and hence the intercept equals the average level of private information (PPI). The regressor is trivially zero at this point, and thereforeβ̂0is not defined (i.e., the regression becomes a trivial one with only an intercept att=0). Second, in the hypothetical case of no pricing errors, it follows thatβ̂t=1andαt̂reveals how much of the informationvremains to be learned. (Note that this case is hypothetical because economic models need noise trading or private-value-based trading as otherwise the no-trade theorem applies (Milgrom and Stokey (1982)). Third, if there exist pricing errors, and if they are orthogonal to the information process (driven bymtandv), then beta is below one and, all else equal, alpha tends to revealvat a slower rate.19
Figure6plots the alpha and beta estimates separately for the tercile of with-wind orders and the tercile of against-wind orders. The with-wind orders are largely information-motivated (see SectionIV.B), and thus the plot should reveal the speed of learning. The against-wind orders are largely uninformed (again SectionIV.B) and therefore serve as a benchmark. We run the regressions withtvarying from zero to four hours. To keep a balanced sample, all of these regressions are run for the subsample of orders that last at least four hours.
The estimated alpha and beta patterns lead to a couple of observations. First, the alpha estimates for the with-wind orders start at about 16 basis points, which is equal to the average PPI for these orders. The private signal is revealed slowly over time, and after about two hours, one-third appears to be revealed. Alpha keeps declining gradually thereafter, but becomes statistically insignificant. Half of the private information seems to be revealed after about three hours. This slow revelation could explain why HFTs need seven hours to detect the institutional order and trade with-wind on it (see SectionIII). For against-wind orders, the alpha estimates are insignificant, likely because these orders are largely uninformed.
Second, the beta estimates are often significantly below one in the early hours, but after three and half hours, they are statistically indistinguishable from one. The other salient feature is that they fall further below one for with-wind orders. These patterns suggest that pricing errors are relatively large in the early hours, and more so for with-wind orders. The stronger pattern for with-wind orders suggests that investors seem to trade very carefully on their information and let pricing errors grow to obfuscate their signal. Alternatively, for against-wind orders, more of the long-term price impact is public information, which raises the covariance betweenmtandvand thus brings beta closer to one, all else equal.
In sum, these results are consistent with the first prediction that investors trade stealthily, so that prices are slow to reveal their private information.
The regressions in SectionIV.Bshow that with-wind trading by HFTs correlates with how informed institutional orders are, and the unbiasedness regressions in SectionV.Bshow that this information is revealed in prices very slowly. In spite of such slow revelation, HFTs appear to be able to detect these orders and trade along with them. But how skilled are these HFTs? In particular, when do they start trading with-wind on these informed orders? Moreover, does HFT net flow evolve differently over time for informed versus uninformed orders?
Figure7plots HFT cumulative net flow over the lifetime of institutional orders split into terciles based on their informational content as measured by PPI. The figure is essentially a 3D version of the signature plot in Figure2, with information on the additional axis. Sell orders are pooled with buy orders by converting them into pseudo-buy orders.
The figure shows that HFTs appear to trade with-wind only on the most informed of long-lasting orders. HFTs' cumulative net flow reaches its highest level after about 15 hours for the tercile of high-PPI (i.e., most informed) orders. The regression results, reported below this graph, reveal that these levels are statistically significant. They turn significantly positive after 10 hours of trading. The with-wind levels are substantially lower for the other terciles and, more importantly, are statistically insignificant. Finally, note that for all three terciles, HFTs start out leaning against the wind in the early hours. This against-wind trading is statistically significant, albeit marginally so for the high-PPI tercile. After between five and seven hours, all terciles show positive levels, which is consistent with the turning point reported in Figure2.20
The evidence presented here is consistent with the second prediction. There is cross-sectional heterogeneity in the long-lasting orders. In particular, some seem clearly more information-motivated, and these are the orders on which HFTs trade significantly with-wind.
Given that HFTs appear able to detect informed orders, several questions arise. First, are investors aware of such detection risk? If so, do they decelerate trading after being detected? And, is their trading before detection consistent with trading off higher speculative profit against greater detection risk? Such a trade-off is admittedly hard to identify in the data. However, if investors' predetection trading is more intense than their overall trading and if they reduce this intensity after being detected, then it would be likely that investors engage in strategic trading in full awareness of HFT detection risk. Such a pattern is predicted by Pred3.
To study trading before and after detection, the trading period of with-wind orders is split into two subperiods, namely, before and after detection by HFTs. We identify detection as the time, going backward from the end of a with-wind order, when HFT cumulative net flow crossed zero. The pre- and postdetection periods are labeled pre-WW and post-WW, respectively. We characterize pre-WW and post-WW trading by generating three sets of trade statistics. The first focuses on how the institutional investor traded, the second characterizes overall trading, and the third zooms in on trading by HFTs. These trade variables are also computed for the overall sample (i.e., including against-wind orders) to provide benchmark values.
The results are presented in TableVI. Several observations stand out. First, the investor statistics suggest that institutional investors do trade intensively in the pre-WW period. For example, in that period, an investor trades 6,530 shares per five minutes, which is about 50% higher than the investor overall average. This higher volume rate is driven by both trading more often and trading in larger sizes. Pre-WW, the investor trades six instead of four times per five minutes, and the trade size is 2,770 shares pre-WW against the overall average of 1,600 shares per trade. However, this intense trading does not lead to an elevated participation in volume.21An investor's participation pre-WW is about 20% compared to 24% overall.22Finally, the more intense pre-WW trading is further evidenced by the extent to which trades were done by market orders (as opposed to passive limit orders): 41% instead of 40%.23Incidentally, this small variation could help explain why this attribute turns insignificant in explaining PPI in TableV.
Second, institutional investors seem to decelerate strongly after HFTs turn to trade along with them. Comparing their pre- and post-WW trading, one notices that an investor trades significantly less intensively on all trade measures. In particular, the investor's volume declines by 54%, trade frequency by 44%, trade size by 53%, volume participation by 48%, and the fraction of market orders by 5%.
Third, we measure the extent to which investors actively manage execution by benchmarking against the commonly used volume-weighted-average-price (VWAP) algorithm. The perfect implementation of VWAP keeps the volume participation rate flat throughout the execution period. In other words, the standard deviation of this rate sampled, say, every five minutes is expected to be zero. In the overall sample, this standard deviation is about 9%, which we do not consider excessive given a mean participation rate of 24% (i.e., institutional investors seem to resort to a VWAP strategy often). Interestingly, pre-WW, this standard deviation increases to 14%, before dropping significantly back to 9% post-WW. We consider this apparent strong deviation from VWAP in the pre-WW period as consistent with the investor optimally trading on a private signal (as evidenced by the slow revelation result of SectionV.B).
Fourth, HFTs trade more aggressively after detection. Their relative number of trades through market orders increases significantly from 41% to 46%. Their participation in overall volume does not change significantly when comparing post-WW with pre-WW. Finally, they appear to resort to market orders to quickly change their position from against-wind to with-wind.
Incidentally, this analysis of pre-WW versus post-WW trading could help explain why the results in this subsection are stronger than the exploratory analysis in TableIV. The latter analysis relates with-wind trading by HFTs to explanatory variables measured over theentirelifetime of the order. Pre- and post-WW trading are then lumped together. Strong pre-WW trading by investors, for example, is then moderated by their weak post-WW trading.
These results provide some empirical support consistent with the third prediction. Investors appear to be aware of detection, as they decelerate their trading strongly after HFTs turn to with-trading. But they also seem to willingly take on this risk, probably to raise their speculative profit, as they trade more intensively prior to being detected—predetection, they trade more often and in larger size.
The final prediction states that detection of an institutional investor's informed orders by HFTs is costly to the investor, and profitable to HFTs. This completes the characterization of the investor-HFT interplay as the investor trades off costly detection against profitable speculation. To test this prediction empirically, we compute the (conditional) correlations between HFT with-wind trading and both the implementation shortfall on investor orders and HFT gross trading revenue.
Before turning to regressions to compute conditional correlations, we first plot the bivariate relationship for both variables by the type of HFT trading. Figure8separates the sample by HFT cumulative net flow tercile (similar to Figure5) and plots the full-sample average and averages for the against-wind and with-wind terciles. As can be seen, the implementation shortfall is four basis points below average for against-wind flow and nine basis points above average for with-wind flow.24The strongly positive association between with-wind trading and transaction costs is consistent with the conjectured trade-off. We caution that this effect is likely to be overstated, whereas the effect of against-wind is understated because of endogeneity. These biases result from reverse causality relationships. For example, against-wind trading causally reduces price pressure in inventory models, but in the data, this effect might be dampened by reverse causality. The reason is that large price pressure makes against-wind trading more profitable, which may generate apositivecorrelation between the two. For with-wind trading, this dynamic works in the other direction. If the price impact is large, then it is more likely to be detected by HFTs, reinforcing the causal effect from with-wind trading to implementation shortfall.
The right-hand side plot of Figure8shows that HFTs' gross trading revenue is positive overall and is positive for both the against-wind and the with-wind terciles individually. HFTs seem to generate most revenue when trading with-wind—$700 per order, which is about twice the overall average.
TableVIIcomplements Figure8by presentingconditionalcorrelations. It again relates implementation shortfall and HFT gross trading revenue by the type of trading by HFTs, but this time adds control variables. The latter are either variables that further characterize execution of the meta order (e.g., duration of the order) or market characteristics over the lifetime of the order (e.g., volatility). All variables are standardized and therefore results become conditional correlations. The variables of interest—implementation shortfall, HFT gross trading revenue, and HFT against/with-wind trading—are expressed either in dollars or in relative terms for robustness.
The results lead to the following additional insights. First, conditionally with-wind trading is also positively correlated with implementation shortfall. The effect is highly statistically significant (i.e., beyond the 1% level). The same holds for the conditional correlation between with-wind trading and HFT gross trading profit. Taken together, these results provide robust evidence of a positive association between with-wind trading and the two variables of interest: investor transaction costs and HFTs' gross trading profit. These results constitute perhaps our strongest support for the fourth prediction.
Second, against-wind trading is not significantly correlated with either implementation-shortfall or HFT-revenue. The signs are consistent with Figure8, but there simply is no statistical significance.
Third, the control variables that are statistically significant in the implementation-shortfall regressions mostly confirm our priors. Transaction costs correlate positively with the relative size of the order, with the extent to which trades are conducted through market orders (as opposed to limit orders), and with market volatility. These signs are all consistent with the microstructure literature. The surprising results are that implementation shortfall is larger for buy orders and for high-turnover periods. These results are statistically the weakest ones, though, with a confidence level of only 90%.
Fourth, the two variables that turn statistically significant in the HFT-revenue regressions are intuitive. HFTs earn higher gross profits when turnover is higher (so that they can off-load inventory easier) and their gross profit margin is higher when the market is more concentrated on the incumbent venue (to probably make up for the higher fees).25
article.VI. Robustness:
In this section, we discuss results of various robustness tests. The results are reported to the Internet Appendix.
An alternative way to run the clock to study how HFTs trade around institutional orders is to use volume instead of calendar time. Volume time increments the clock by, for example, an hour when market volume has incremented by 1/8.5 of a stock's average daily volume (a trading day in Stockholm lasts 8.5 hours). We then track HFT cumulative net flow on this new time grid. To avoid issues of concatenating trades across days, we run this analysis only for the first day of an order's execution period.26Figure IA.5 in the Internet Appendix shows that the HFT trading pattern in volume time echoes the pattern in calendar time of the signature plot of Figure2. In particular, HFTs appear to trade against-wind initially and with-wind eventually on long-lasting orders.
The with-wind pattern is somewhat weaker than in the calendar time analysis and this might be due to the following. There is with-wind trading on some orders that are executed in periods of low market volume. These orders by definition are short-lasting involumetime. They also have a high participation rate, which makes hiding more difficult, and which explains why one sees a lot of with-wind flow. However, such orders leave the sample quickly in a volume time analysis. During periods of low volume, the investor may rationally choose to take on more detection risk if his clock ticks in natural time. For example, his information advantage dissipates in calendar time when the trading day finishes (and overnight news arrives that makes his private signal redundant), or when a public news announcement comes closer.
To study whether with-wind trading is an end-of-day-phenomenon, we run the following additional analysis. First, we plot the dispersion of order start and end times of the with-wind subsample in Figure IA.6 in the Internet Appendix. The figure shows that these times are dispersed throughout the day in a way similar to the full sample (see Figure IA.1). Further analysis reveals that the HFT trading pattern is insensitive to whether the order ends in the final trading hour or not. Figure IA.7 in the Internet Appendix reruns the signature plot after splitting the sample based on whether an order stopped executing before the final trading hour. The general pattern of against-wind initially and then with-wind trading eventually for long-lasting orders holds for both subsamples. These results suggest that our main result is not an end-of-day phenomenon.
The NASDAQ OMX market share of the exchange-traded volume was 65% at the time of our sample. The HFT cumulative net flow analysis is based on NASDAQ OMX only because this was the sole exchange that reported the trader's identity for each trade (at the end of the trading day). If HFTs trade randomly across exchanges, then the NASDAQ OMX subsample is representative and all of the results go through. If not, NASDAQ OMX is likely to be the exchange whose majority of trades execute simply because it is the exchange with the largest market share. This line of thought inspired us to conduct a robustness check where we select only orders on those stock-days when the NASDAQ OMX market share was beyond some threshold and then rerun the main analysis. The largest market share we can select while still having sufficient observations for statistical inference is 70%, which reduces the sample from 5,136 orders to 863 orders. We confirm statistical significance of our main result in Figure IA.8: HFTs initially trade against-wind but eventually trade with-wind for long-lasting orders.27
Unfortunately, because we cannot go beyond the 70% cutoff level, incomplete HFT data remain a concern, but we are not overly worried for the following reasons. First, if the NASDAQ HFT position were perfectly offset by opposite positions in non-NASDAQ markets, then the HFT NASDAQ position would be economically meaningless, or just “noise,” and this would work against us when trying to establish empirical patterns. The fact that we find statistically significant patterns therefore suggests that the HFT NASDAQ position is not pure noise. Second, Table IA.I in the Internet Appendix shows that HFT directionality (on NASDAQ) approaches zero when it is sampled at lower frequencies. HFTs appear to mean-revert at least some of their NASDAQ position across days, which they would not need to do if they had perfectly offsetting positions in non-NASDAQ markets. Taken together, these arguments suggest that our HFT cumulative net flow variable is potentially noisy but carries signals as well.
Although we find that HFTs collectively trade against-wind after an investor's first child trade, one could argue that they might have traded with-windaheadof the execution of this first child trade. If the investor polled interest in, for example, dark pools or submitted a nonmarketable limit order on an exchange, then this signal could have been picked up by HFTs, prompting them to trade with-wind immediately after receiving such signal. In this case, the against-wind flow after the first child trade could simply be due to the unwinding of a predatory with-wind position. We do not observe investor orders (we observe only their trades), so we cannot test this hypothesis directly. Instead, we start tracking HFT cumulative net flow one hour before an investor's first child trade. Figure IA.10 shows that this flow is small and statistically insignificant. We therefore consider this alternative interpretation highly unlikely (since HFTs must have picked up a signal, if any, at least an hour before an investor's first trade).
article.VII. Conclusion:
In this paper, we find that HFTs trade along with institutional orders—with the wind—if such orders last at least a few hours. Additional analysis suggests that in these cases, investor trading is information-motivated and HFTs try to detect such trading in order to join it. Such HFT with-wind trading is costly to investors and profitable to HFTs.
We also find that HFTs appear to need considerable time to detect these informed orders (several hours), which appears at odds with their typical characterization as ultra-high-speed traders. We believe that HFTs should be more broadly characterized as high-tech traders. Technology enables them to trade at submicrosecond speed, and also endows them with an ability to parse “big data” to detect informed trading across several hours (such a period becomes big data if one includes all message traffic for the security at hand and all related securities).
How do our findings relate to market quality? One might argue that HFT trading with-wind on institutional orders speeds up price discovery. In other words, HFTs trading in the same direction as informed investors could make prices reveal investors' private information more quickly. This might make prices more efficient in the short run. The worrisome side effect is that, in the long run, prices could becomelessefficient. Institutional investors could discontinue costly analyst research, since informational rents have to be shared with others in the trading process, that is, research might no longer be privately profitable. This could become socially costly if informational externalities are large (i.e., information benefits the allocation of capital across entrepreneurs).
We believe that the market structure debate should recenter on end-user costs. Data are hard to come by, but it should be in the interest of end users—retail and institutional investors—to make their trade data available. Alternatively, regulators could demand the reporting of more granular data, in the spirit of U.S. regulatory changes after the 1987 crash. Exchanges were required to identify retail orders in the consolidated equity audit trail data. For each trade they completed, brokers had to report whether it was a principal or an agency trade and, for agency trades, whether it was for a retail investor or an institutional investor. This enabled more analysis to inform future debates on market quality. The recent SEC initiative to amend Rule 613 and create a consolidated audit tape (for regulatory use only) seems like a step in the right direction.28
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
